problem_addressed: Computer-aided diagnosis systems are trained on oversimplified one-hot labels that ignore diagnostic uncertainty, leading to overconfident predictions especially for noisy, ambiguous, or artifact-laden medical images.
core_claim: By leveraging neural network training dynamics to generate calibrated uncertainty-aware pseudo-labels, we can improve uncertainty estimation and robustness in medical image classification.
key_contributions: Introduction of a framework that brings uncertainty back into the label space using neural network training dynamics
Development of calibrated pseudo-label generation methods (Pseudo-T and Pseudo-D)
Demonstration of superior performance on echocardiography classification benchmark
novelty: Using neural network training dynamics (model predictions over time during training) to assess sample difficulty and generate uncertainty-aware pseudo-labels, rather than relying on traditional hard labels.
method_summary: The method leverages neural network training dynamics to assess sample difficulty by aggregating model predictions over training epochs, then generates uncertainty-aware pseudo-labels using temperature scaling and Dirichlet calibration approaches.
method_components: Neural Network Training Dynamics (NNTD) - recording and aggregating model predictions over training epochs
Pseudo-label generation using averaged softmax predictions
Calibration of pseudo-labels using temperature scaling and Dirichlet calibration
key_innovation: Using the temporal evolution of model predictions during training (training dynamics) as a source of information about sample difficulty and uncertainty, which is then encoded into calibrated pseudo-labels.
assumptions: Model predictions over training epochs reflect sample difficulty
Aggregated predictions can serve as a proxy for uncertainty
Calibration on validation set will generalize to training set
theoretical_grounding: The paper references prior work showing that training on soft targets improves resistance to label noise and enhances uncertainty estimation. The calibration methods (temperature scaling and Dirichlet calibration) are based on established techniques in the literature.
datasets_used: aortic stenosis classification dataset (ultrasound videos of same patient)
hyperparameters: R(2+1)D model with Kinetics400 initialization, ADAM optimizer, learning rate 1e-4 for 20 epochs, random rotation ±15°, cropping ratio 0.7, temperature parameters γ, A and b tuned using SGD with learning rate 0.01 and λ1 = λ2 = 1
headline_result: Pseudo-D achieves best performance: Study-level MAE ↓ 0.180, ACC ↑ 0.820, ECE ↓ 0.071, AURC ↑ 0.885
ablations: Comparison between Pseudo-T and Pseudo-D shows that class-wise calibration parameters improve performance
Pseudo-D includes class-specific scaling factors while Pseudo-T lacks class-wise calibration
improvement_over_baseline: Pseudo-D improves over Vanilla baseline: Study-level MAE reduced by 19.6% (0.224→0.180), ACC increased by 3.8% (0.790→0.820), ECE reduced by 48.6% (0.138→0.071), AURC increased by 3.9% (0.852→0.885); Video-level MAE reduced by 14.4% (0.257→0.220), ACC increased by 2.9% (0.765→0.787), ECE reduced by 43.5% (0.170→0.096), AURC increased by 1.4% (0.804→0.793)
stated_limitations: Focus is limited to medical images, which may limit generalizability to other domains
Relies on neural network training dynamics, which might be computationally expensive
unstated_weaknesses: Lack of details on specific calibration methods used
No comparison with existing uncertainty estimation techniques
No discussion of computational efficiency or scalability
Lack of information about specific types of medical images used
No mention of how the method handles class imbalance in medical datasets
threats_to_validity: Generalization risk: may not work well for different types of images
Effectiveness depends on how well neural network training dynamics reflect true uncertainty
No mention of how uncertainty is evaluated or validated
open_questions: How does the method perform across different medical imaging modalities?
What is the computational overhead of incorporating neural network training dynamics?
How does the method compare to other uncertainty estimation approaches?
What are the optimal aggregation and calibration strategies for different types of medical data?
future_work: Extending the method to other medical imaging modalities
Comparing with other uncertainty estimation techniques
Incorporating domain knowledge into the uncertainty estimation process
gaps_this_enables: New research directions in uncertainty-aware pseudo-labeling for medical imaging
Potential for more reliable computer-aided diagnosis systems
Opportunities to improve model calibration in medical applications
Possibility of integrating expert knowledge into the uncertainty estimation process
relevance_to_topic: This paper directly addresses how to generate pseudo-labels that account for uncertainty in medical imaging, which is a key aspect of uncertainty-aware pseudo-label selection.
reusable_assets: Method for assessing sample difficulty using neural network training dynamics
Approach for aggregating and calibrating model predictions
best_result: Pseudo-D achieves best performance: Study-level MAE ↓ 0.180, ACC ↑ 0.820, ECE ↓ 0.071, AURC ↑ 0.885
limitations_stated: Focus is limited to medical images, which may limit generalizability to other domains
Relies on neural network training dynamics, which might be computationally expensive
title: Pseudo-D: Informing Multi-View Uncertainty Estimation with Calibrated Neural Training Dynamics
